pages.text:
CHARCOAT IC - FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS
WHAT IS CHARCOAT IC
CharCoat IC is a RTV (Room Temperature Vulcanizing) silicone hydrophobic coa(cid:415)ng which is a specialized coa(cid:415)ng
applied to surfaces to create a water-repellent (hydrophobic) layer. This coa(cid:415)ng is typically composed of silicone elas-
tomers that cure at room temperature to form a rubber-like, flexible protec(cid:415)ve layer.
When applied to electrical insulators, RTV silicone coa(cid:415)ngs offer several benefits:
A. Hydrophobicity: The silicone surface repels water, preven(cid:415)ng the forma(cid:415)on of con(cid:415)nuous water films on the insu-
lator's surface. This reduces the risk of water-related conduc(cid:415)vity issues, such as leakage currents and flashovers.
B. Contamina(cid:415)on Resistance: RTV silicone coa(cid:415)ngs can maintain their hydrophobic proper(cid:415)es even in polluted envi-
ronments, where contaminants like dust, salt, or industrial pollutants would otherwise accumulate. The coa(cid:415)ng
causes contaminants to bead up and be washed away by rain or wind, maintaining the insulator’s performance.
C. Improved Electrical Performance: By minimizing the forma(cid:415)on of conduc(cid:415)ve water films and reducing surface
contamina(cid:415)on, RTV silicone coa(cid:415)ngs help reduce leakage currents and arcing, extending the lifespan of the insulator
and improving overall electrical reliability.
D. Self-Healing: CharCoat IC RTV silicone coa(cid:415)ng possess “Silanes” which have self-healing proper(cid:415)es, meaning they
can restore their hydrophobic characteris(cid:415)cs even a(cid:332)er exposure to environmental stress, making them ideal for
outdoor high-voltage applica(cid:415)ons.
In summary, RTV silicone hydrophobic coa(cid:415)ngs protect electrical insulators by improving their resistance to water
and pollu(cid:415)on, enhancing their electrical performance, and reducing maintenance needs.
TECHNICAL
1. Volumetric Resis(cid:415)vity
The Volumetric Resis(cid:415)vity of CharCoat IC at 2.29 x 1015 ohm.cm well exceeds the typically required value specified
of 3 x 1013 ohm.cm.
2. Surface Resis(cid:415)vity
The Surface Resis(cid:415)vity specified as “4 x 10 ohm.cm” is not meaningful. CharCoat IC provides a Surface Resis(cid:415)vity of
1.5 x 1013 ohms.
3. Viscosity
The viscosity of CharCoat IC is around 325cP. This is the reason that it is so easy to apply and yields a smooth, even
finish which is far superior to other products. The excellent surface quality achieved with CharCoat IC is a result of
the formula(cid:415)on which u(cid:415)lizes a blend of solvents with differing evapora(cid:415)on rates.
With more viscous coa(cid:415)ngs, addi(cid:415)onal solvent o(cid:332)en needs to be added during the course of the applica(cid:415)on process
as the material becomes too thick. CharCoat IC, with its lower viscosity, completely eliminates the need to handle
addi(cid:415)onal hazardous materials while on site.
4. Skin-over Time
The Skin-over and Curing (cid:415)mes of silicone rubbers is dependent on the ambient temperature.
CharCoat IC will normally become touch-dry within twenty minutes of applica(cid:415)on.

pages.text:
5. Dissipa(cid:415)on Factor
Being a thin coa(cid:415)ng sprayed onto various insula(cid:415)ng substrates this is difficult to measure and not cri(cid:415)cal to the func-
(cid:415)oning of the material. The Dielectric Strength is more important and CharCoat IC provides an excep(cid:415)onal break-
down voltage of 64kV/mm.
6. Tensile Strength
Once applied to an insulator surface, the coa(cid:415)ng is not subjected to any mechanical stress. For informa(cid:415)on, however,
the tensile strength of CharCoat IC is approximately 6 MPa.
7. Elonga(cid:415)on at Break
As above, it is not really relevant, but the elonga(cid:415)on for CharCoat IC is approximately 500%
8. Tear Strength
As above, it is not really relevant, but the tear strength of CharCoat IC is approximately 25kN/m.
9. Material Density
The density of CharCoat IC is 1000kg/m3.
10. Volumetric Solids Ra(cid:415)o
The percentage solids by weight is a somewhat meaningless figure – it is dictated by the quan(cid:415)ty of solvent present
in the material. As the solvent evaporates, 100% solids are le(cid:332) on the insulator. It thus has absolutely no effect on the
performance of the coa(cid:415)ng. Moreover, with lower solvent contents, “thinning/dilu(cid:415)on is o(cid:332)en needed during appli-
ca(cid:415)on – then the percentage solids are reduced from the “as supplied” value.
A far more important parameter is the ra(cid:415)o filler and other solids to the silicone content. The tendency is for manu-
facturers to include too much filler because it is significantly less expensive than the silicone and can improve the
results of ar(cid:415)ficial tracking and erosion tests which inten(cid:415)onally destroy the hydrophobicity of the coa(cid:415)ng. Obvious-
ly, under service condi(cid:415)ons, the longer hydrophobicity recovery and transfer (cid:415)mes caused by lower silicone content,
adversely affect the coa(cid:415)ng performance.
In our opinion, the ra(cid:415)o of silicone rubber to other solids content should be at least 55:45, and ideally 60:40.
11. Mass Solids Ra(cid:415)o of Material
Comments as above.
12. Coa(cid:415)ng Adhesion
Silicone rubber does not naturally adhere to other materials – hence its widespread use in the manufacture of
moulds. In view of this, most coa(cid:415)ngs contain significant volumes of bonding agent. This means that the percentage
of silicone in the coa(cid:415)ng is reduced and, moreover, the bulk of the bonding material is dispersed within the coa(cid:415)ng
layer where it serves no beneficial purpose.
For CharCoat IC, a separate primer is provided for spraying onto the insulator prior to applica(cid:415)on of the coa(cid:415)ng.
Therefore, the bonding agent exists only where it is required, i.e. at the interface between the coa(cid:415)ng and the porce-
lain, and no extraneous material is displacing the silicone content.
As illustrated in our bonding Test Report, a(cid:332)er a 12 cycle,100 hour boiling test, the specimen with a pre-applied
primer achieves far be(cid:425)er adhesion than when 10% by weight of bonding agent is added to the coa(cid:415)ng or when no
primer or bonding agent is used. Further, in the “cross-hatch” test specified in ISO 16276-2, CharCoat IC achieves
class 1 adhesion.

pages.text:
5. Insulator Cleaning
This depends en(cid:415)rely on the environment that the equipment finds itself in. This is why pollu(cid:415)on severity measure-
ment is such an important parameter. We had/have marine sites that needed cleaning or greasing every 6 months
which in it self is really not prac(cid:415)cal, or industrial pollu(cid:415)on that needed a(cid:425)en(cid:415)on every year or every second year.
Which ever way you look at it, they are short term solu(cid:415)ons and coa(cid:415)ng is the way to go because you at least buy
yourself 10 years maintenance free.
6. Coa(cid:415)ng vs Mainteance
The main value is definitely financial because you are comparing 10 years versus 6, 12 or 24 months maintenance.
Remember that the maintenance normally requires outages and the mobilisa(cid:415)on of many staff members and their
equipment and accommoda(cid:415)on. These costs add up over the 10 year period.
7. Creepage
Dielectric strength is measured as the maximum voltage required to produce a dielectric breakdown through a mate-
rial. So if the creepage goes up so does the dielectric strength, and vice versa. This must not be confused with the
breakdown of air between two components.
8. Applica(cid:415)on
Please refer to our CharCoat IC Applica(cid:415)on guide for full infom-
ra(cid:415)on.
CharCoat IC is designed to be applied over suitably prepared and
cleaned insulators.
A thorough cleaning of the insulator is required. If the surface is
just dusty or if close to the ocean with plain salt deposits,
normal clean water will suffice for cleaning. If, however, the
insulators are seriously contaminated we recommend Toluene
or Xylene for cleaning down.
Spray Guns and Tips
Recommend 208 airless tip for the CharCoat IC Primer at 60 bar
and a more than satisfactory finish with a 412 tip at 100 bar for
the CharCoat IC coating using a Contractor Inline gun or similar.
Alternatively, application can be completed on site with com-
pressors and High-Pressure hoses and spray guns.

Which ever way you look at it, they are short term solu(cid:415)ons and coa(cid:415)ng is the way to go because you at least buy yourself 10 years maintenance free. 6. Coa(cid:415)ng vs Mainteance The main value is definitely financial because you are comparing 10 years versus 6, 12 or 24 months maintenance. Remember that the maintenance normally requires outages and the mobilisa(cid:415)on of many staff members and their equipment and accommoda(cid:415)on. These costs add up over the 10 year period. 7. Creepage Dielectric strength is measured as the maximum voltage required to produce a dielectric breakdown through a mate- rial. So if the creepage goes up so does the dielectric strength, and vice versa. This must not be confused with the breakdown of air between two components. 8. Applica(cid:415)on Please refer to our CharCoat IC Applica(cid:415)on guide for full infom- ra(cid:415)on. CharCoat IC is designed to be applied over suitably prepared and cleaned insulators. A thorough cleaning of the insulator is required. If the surface is just dusty or if close to the ocean with plain salt deposits, normal clean water will suffice for cleaning. If, however, the insulators are seriously contaminated we recommend Toluene or Xylene for cleaning down. Spray Guns and Tips Recommend 208 airless tip for the CharCoat IC Primer at 60 bar and a more than satisfactory finish with a 412 tip at 100 bar for the CharCoat IC coating using a Contractor Inline gun or similar.
